key_insight: Lunar south-pole surface charging is strongly controlled by both local terrain shielding and the surrounding plasma environment, producing large potential and electric-field variations across craters and uplands.
engineering_relevance: These charging maps can guide safe landing-site selection, rover route planning, and electrostatic protection design for lunar infrastructure and surface hardware.
method: Built a high-fidelity lunar south-pole terrain model from optimized LRO/LOLA elevation data (86°S–90°S).
Simulated charging over half a lunar orbital cycle with a finite element–BP neural network approach.
Used lunar-phase-dependent plasma inputs spanning solar wind and multiple Earth magnetospheric regions.
evidence_signal: The simulations show potential decreases toward about -1000 V in the plasma sheet and peak electric fields of roughly 5 V/m, with strong terrain-dependent differences across crater rims and walls.
watch_item: The results are simulation-based and depend on the fidelity of terrain reconstruction and plasma boundary inputs, so local predictions may require in situ validation.
paper_title: Influence of Magnetospheric Plasma Environment on Surface Charging of the Lunar South Pole
